column3: Could you briefly introduce yourself and your academic background?
column4: What motivated you to pursue IDDDP/DD?
column5: Can you briefly describe your research project and the work you carried out?
column6: What technical and personal skills did you gain through your research experience?
column7: How has the programme influenced your academic journey and future career plans?
column8: Did your research lead to any publications, presentations, awards, or other notable outcomes?
column9: What were some of the biggest challenges you faced during your journey, and how did you overcome them?
column10: What advice would you give to students who are considering this programme?

column3: Samuel pursued the Dual Degree programme in Chemistry with a strong interest in research and a desire to explore academia beyond undergraduate studies. The experience not only strengthened his technical skills but also helped him gain clarity about his long-term goals. Having completed his degree requirements, he is currently awaiting the results of his PhD applications.
column4: For Samuel, the Dual Degree programme was an opportunity to understand whether research was the right path for him. While he initially expected research to resemble undergraduate projects, he soon realised that it required a different mindset—one built on patience, consistency, and independent problem-solving. The programme allowed him to experience these challenges firsthand while exploring his academic interests in greater depth.
column5:
Samuel's research focused on automating cell tracking and segmentation in microscopic images collected over long durations. Such work has important applications in biological and cancer research, where understanding cellular behaviour over time can provide valuable scientific insights.

Working under Prof. Sandip Kar, Samuel improved an existing cell-segmentation framework known as Cellpose by introducing modifications designed for long-duration tracking. These enhancements resulted in roughly a 10% improvement in performance. One of the most rewarding aspects of the project was finding ways to improve results despite limited computational resources, highlighting the role of creativity in research.
column6:
Through the project, Samuel gained experience in machine learning, image segmentation, cellular data analysis, and computational modelling. He also worked extensively with Python and biological data-processing tools.

Beyond technical skills, the experience improved his ability to communicate complex ideas, present interdisciplinary research, and respond to feedbacks. These lessons helped him become a more confident and effective researcher.
column7:
The programme played a major role in shaping Samuel's future plans. More than any technical outcome, it helped him realise that he wanted to continue pursuing research and eventually undertake a PhD.

The experience also introduced him to areas involving computational biology and data-driven research, helping him identify topics that genuinely interested him while developing a deeper understanding of academic research.
column8: Although the work had not yet been formally published at the time of the interview, Samuel hopes to publish the findings in the future. The improvements achieved during the project demonstrate its potential impact within the field.
column9:
One of the biggest challenges Samuel faced was conducting interdisciplinary research as a Chemistry student working extensively with machine learning techniques. Communicating the value of computational approaches to different audiences often required additional effort.

Resource limitations also posed challenges, but they ultimately encouraged him to think more creatively and develop efficient solutions.
column10:
Samuel encourages students who are genuinely curious about research to consider the Dual Degree programme, particularly if they are unsure about pursuing a PhD. The experience provides valuable exposure to the realities of academic research while helping students discover their interests.

For students working on interdisciplinary projects, Samuel recommends seeking guidance from experts across multiple fields and remaining open to learning from different perspectives. According to him, persistence, adaptability, and curiosity are just as important as technical ability.

column3: Ameya Marakarkandy chose the Aerospace Engineering Dual Degree programme as an opportunity to continue pursuing a problem he was already deeply invested in through the IIT Bombay Student Satellite Program. Following graduation, Ameya plans to continue working on his satellite technology startup and the ideas that took shape during his research journey.
column4:
For Ameya, the decision to convert from B.Tech to the Dual Degree programme was driven by a clear goal. Having already begun working on satellite-related projects, he saw the programme as an opportunity to gain additional time to develop both his technical expertise and entrepreneurial ambitions.

Rather than viewing the programme solely as an academic pursuit, Ameya used it to explore a long-term challenge that aligned closely with his vision for the future of space technology.
column5:
Ameya's project emerged from the IIT Bombay Student Satellite Program and focused on technologies that could enable satellites to be serviced and maintained in orbit. Such capabilities have the potential to make future space missions more sustainable and cost-effective.

Under the guidance of Prof. Hemendra Arya, Ameya worked on mission design and systems engineering for a CubeSat mission aimed at demonstrating proximity operations and docking between satellites. His contributions centred on guidance, navigation, and control systems, which are critical for enabling complex satellite interactions.
column6:
The project exposed Ameya to a wide range of aerospace engineering disciplines, including systems engineering, mission design, and guidance, navigation, and control. He also gained experience with tools such as MATLAB, Python, ANSYS, and System Composer.

Equally valuable was learning how to approach large engineering problems systematically. Working across multiple subsystems strengthened his problem-solving abilities and reinforced the importance of iterative design and testing.
column7:
The Dual Degree programme gave Ameya the opportunity to continue pursuing a problem he genuinely cared about. The additional time allowed him to remain deeply involved with satellite development while refining ideas connected to his startup ambitions.

The experience strengthened his commitment to working in the space sector and demonstrated how research and entrepreneurship can complement one another.
column8: The satellite programme has led to several conference presentations and research publications. Ameya contributed as a co-author to some of these works, providing opportunities to share the team's findings with the broader space-technology community.
column9:
One of the biggest challenges came during implementation and testing. While solutions often appeared straightforward on paper, real-world testing frequently revealed issues that required multiple rounds of refinement.

Ameya also found the conversion process somewhat difficult to navigate, relying heavily on conversations with seniors and administrative offices to understand the requirements.
column10:
Ameya believes the Dual Degree programme is particularly valuable for students who want to develop expertise in a specialised field or pursue ambitious long-term projects. However, he emphasises the importance of having a clear reason for choosing the programme.

His advice is to speak with seniors, understand the requirements thoroughly, and explore potential project opportunities before making a decision. For students with a strong technical interest, the additional year can be an invaluable opportunity for growth.

column3: Saksham began his journey in Mechanical Engineering before converting to C-MInDS after discovering a growing interest in artificial intelligence, machine learning, and software. Today, he works at Qualcomm in a AI integrated software role, a career path that reflects the shift in interests that began during his time at IIT Bombay.
column4:
By the end of his second year, Saksham realised that his interests were moving away from traditional mechanical engineering and towards computing and intelligent systems. Rather than continuing down a path that no longer excited him, he began exploring alternative academic options.

C-MInDS stood out because of its interdisciplinary nature and flexibility. The programme exposed him to a range of topics within AI and data science, allowing him to explore different areas before identifying the ones that interested him most.
column5:
Saksham's Dual Degree project focused on developing a reinforcement learning-based controller for a soft robotic arm. The goal was to replace an existing mathematical model with a learning-based system that could adapt more effectively to the challenges of controlling soft robotic structures.

Working under Prof. Abhishek Gupta and Prof. Shivaram Kalyanakrishnan, Saksham combined concepts from robotics, machine learning, and control systems. The project provided him with an opportunity to see reinforcement learning in action and understand how intelligent systems improve through interaction and feedback.
column6:
The project gave Saksham extensive experience with Python, C++, MATLAB, Arduino, and robotics hardware. Working across both software and hardware systems helped him develop a broader understanding of how complex engineering solutions are built and tested.

Perhaps more importantly, the experience strengthened his ability to independently learn new concepts and adapt to unfamiliar challenges—skills that continue to be valuable in his professional career.
column7:
The programme played a major role in Saksham's transition from Mechanical Engineering to a AI-software-focused career. The combination of coursework, research, and practical projects helped him build skills that aligned closely with opportunities in artificial intelligence and software development.

More importantly, the experience gave him greater clarity about the kinds of problems he enjoyed solving and expanded the range of career opportunities available to him.
column8:
Although the project did not lead to a publication, Saksham considers it a valuable research experience. The challenges encountered during the project limited the results that could be achieved, but the lessons learned from the process proved equally important.

The experience gave him firsthand exposure to the realities of research, including the uncertainty and setbacks that often accompany ambitious projects.
column9:
One of the biggest challenges was the slow pace of experimentation. Because the project relied heavily on hardware testing, opportunities to run experiments were limited, making progress slower than in purely simulation-based work.

Saksham also found it challenging at times to communicate the full complexity of the project during evaluations, where limited presentation time often restricted detailed discussion of the work.
column10:
Saksham encourages students to spend their early years exploring a variety of courses and disciplines before committing to a specific path. In his view, one of the greatest advantages of IIT Bombay is the freedom to discover interests that may lie outside one's original department.

For students considering C-MInDS, his advice is to choose the programme out of genuine interest and curiosity. Saksham's own journey demonstrates how exploring new interests can open unexpected and rewarding opportunities.

column3: Diya K Aravind pursued the Dual Degree (DD) programme in the Department of Chemical Engineering. She is now planning to pursue a PhD in Chemical Engineering, which she will be starting soon. Beyond higher studies, she notes that the opportunities available after the DD programme are broadly similar to those after a B.Tech degree, including industry roles, corporate R&D positions, and other career paths. A key added advantage, she points out, is that holding a Master's degree can strengthen PhD applications- particularly in Europe, where a Master's is often a requirement.
column4: At the time of choosing the programme, Diya was unsure about her next step and wanted more time before committing to a long-term career decision. The DD programme allowed her to earn a Master's degree while gaining additional research experience. There was also a specific project that interested her and that she wanted to explore further.
column5: Diya's project was a CFD simulation of a droplet being smeared by a substrate, carried out under the supervision of Prof. Mahesh Tirumkudulu. The work involved building a simulation/model related to a device developed in the laboratory, with the objective of studying and understanding the behaviour of the system under different conditions.
column6:
Technical skills: Diya learned to use the simulation software COMSOL along with other computational tools relevant to her project work.

Soft skills: The most useful skills she developed were presentation skills and scientific writing. The programme involved a significant amount of both, and she expects these to be highly valuable as she begins her PhD.
column7:
The DD programme played an important role in strengthening Diya's research profile. Opportunities such as conducting independent research and presenting at a conference improved her preparation for pursuing a PhD.

While the programme did not fundamentally change her career plans, it gave her an additional year to explore her interests and gain meaningful research experience. The biggest benefit, in her words, was the exposure to research itself. Unlike a typical B.Tech schedule dominated by coursework and examinations, the DD programme gave her the time to focus on a single problem, think deeply about it, and develop a structured approach to solving it - an experience she considers particularly valuable for anyone interested in research.
column8: The project has already resulted in a conference presentation in December. A manuscript based on the work is currently in preparation.
column9:
The biggest challenge Diya faced was dealing with health issues during the course of the project, which required her to return home for a period of time. Fortunately, her project work could be continued remotely, allowing her to keep making progress despite the circumstances.

Looking back, the one thing she would change is the project allocation. She eventually worked on a project that was not her first preference, largely because there was a need for someone to take it up and she met the eligibility requirements. Given the choice, she would have preferred a project more closely aligned with her original interests.
column10:
The DD programme, Diya believes, is particularly useful for students who are still exploring their future options and are unsure about their immediate next step. It is also highly beneficial for students interested in research, as it offers research experience that is difficult to obtain during a standard B.Tech programme.

Her key piece of advice is to speak to seniors who have already gone through the programme - many important aspects of the experience are best understood through direct conversations with people who have firsthand knowledge.

On host-department-specific concerns, she notes that in departments where very few students pursue the DD route, administrative procedures can sometimes be unclear. Here too, speaking with seniors helps, as they often carry practical knowledge about departmental requirements and procedures.

Finally, before making a decision, she recommends identifying professors whose research aligns with your interests and speaking with them directly. Understanding their research areas and expectations can help you assess whether the project and programme are a good fit for you.

column3: Aakarsh completed the Inter-Disciplinary Dual Degree Programme (IDDDP), moving from Chemistry to SJMSOM (the Shailesh J. Mehta School of Management). On completing the programme, he was eligible for all of the opportunities available to MBA graduates, including roles in consulting, operations, finance, marketing, and other management domains.
column4: At the time, the job market was not particularly attractive, and Aakarsh was certain he did not want to continue in Chemistry as a long-term career. He was exploring alternative options, and an MBA was one of them. Having already spent several years at IIT Bombay and being comfortable with the campus environment, he found the IDDDP an attractive opportunity to earn an MBA within one additional year.
column5: Aakarsh's project, supervised by Prof. Indranil Mukherjee, focused on supply-chain optimization and supplier selection. The objective was to develop an optimization framework that could rank suppliers and determine the ideal allocation of orders among them. The most exciting aspect was working on a problem that closely resembled real-world industry scenarios.
column6: On the technical side, Aakarsh used Minitab, Python, and a range of optimization and analytical methodologies. Beyond the tools, the experience strengthened his quantitative problem-solving skills and his ability to approach operational problems in a structured manner. The programme was also a major source of soft skills - understanding group dynamics, communicating effectively with different stakeholders, adapting his approach to different people, collaborating with teams, and navigating professional situations diplomatically.
column7:
The MBA opened up opportunities in operations and management, allowing Aakarsh to apply the analytical foundation developed during his undergraduate studies in a completely different domain. The programme let him enter the management domain immediately after graduation without having to pursue a separate MBA later, and as a result he was able to secure opportunities in Operations and build a career in that field. More broadly, it expanded the range of career options available to him beyond what a conventional Chemistry degree would typically offer.

The biggest benefit, in his view, was the combination of time and cost savings. Compared to pursuing a separate MBA after graduation, the programme allowed him to save an additional year, obtain an MBA at a significantly lower cost, and continue studying in a familiar environment.
column8: While the project did not result in formal publications or patents, its real value lay in tackling a problem that closely mirrored real-world industry scenarios - building a supplier-ranking and order-allocation framework that gave Aakarsh hands-on, application-driven experience directly relevant to his subsequent career in operations.
column9: The biggest challenge was managing his timetable, as the MBA and undergraduate programmes followed different schedules, academic structures, and lecture timings.
column10:
Aakarsh believes the programme is well suited for students who are certain they want to pursue an MBA immediately after their undergraduate studies, want to save both time and money compared to a standalone MBA later, enjoy working with people and solving business problems, and are interested in moving beyond purely technical roles. Before choosing it, students should have a clear reason for pursuing an MBA immediately rather than after gaining work experience - since many people pursue MBAs after a few years in industry to transition into leadership, management, or people-oriented roles, students should think carefully about why they want to make that transition early and whether it aligns with their long-term goals.

On host-department-specific concerns, he notes that courses from different departments often clash, so students may need to work closely with faculty advisors and department offices to ensure graduation requirements can be completed without unnecessary complications. Among courses, he strongly recommends MG401 (Marketing), which he found extremely engaging and valuable, as well as courses taught by Prof. Narayan Rangaraj - particularly those related to Infrastructure Management and Operations - for their insights into large-scale operational systems.

column3: Arya completed the Inter-Disciplinary Dual Degree Programme (IDDDP), transitioning from Energy Science and Engineering to Electrical Engineering (Electronic Systems), and is currently working as an Analog Design Engineer at Texas Instruments.
column4: The IDDDP gave Arya the chance to formally specialize in Electrical Engineering, moving away from a power-focused trajectory toward analog electronics and IC design.
column5: Arya's project, supervised by Prof. Laxmeesha Somappa, focused on designing a wirelessly powered neuromodulation device. The broader goal was to develop an implantable device capable of stimulating neurons in the brain for the treatment of conditions such as epilepsy and related neurological disorders. Having previously worked mostly at the system level, Arya was introduced through this project to circuit-level and IC-level design, and found the work especially compelling because it addressed a highly novel research problem - at the targeted scale, this type of system had not been extensively developed in India.
column6: The most important tool Arya learned was Cadence Virtuoso, the industry-standard platform for analog and integrated circuit design. Other tools and methodologies included ANSYS HFSS for electromagnetic modelling and simulation, circuit design methodologies, wireless power transfer concepts, analog and IC design workflows, and MATLAB-based analysis and modelling. The most directly useful skill has been proficiency with Cadence Virtuoso, which Arya uses regularly at Texas Instruments, along with valuable exposure to the complete IC design workflow.
column7: Without the programme, Arya would likely have remained focused on power and power electronics. Instead, the IDDDP opened the door to analog electronics, IC design, and related research areas, and directly contributed to the current role at Texas Instruments. The biggest benefit was the degree itself and the opportunities that came with it: in the semiconductor and IC design industry, an Electrical Engineering degree carries significant weight, and the programme made it much easier to access opportunities in analog and integrated circuit design.
column8: The project was connected to a larger research effort and may have involved industry collaboration.
column9: The biggest technical challenge was the steep learning curve associated with analog and IC design, though strong support from senior students and the research group helped. Moving from Energy Science and Engineering to Electrical Engineering also meant initially lacking a network within the new department, and building connections with faculty, students, and researchers took time.
column10:
Arya advises considering the programme only after gaining some exposure to the field you want to transition into, and strongly recommends taking relevant courses, working on projects, and exploring the field before applying. Students should understand the course requirements, specialization electives, and graduation requirements before making the transition, since there are only a limited number of semesters to complete everything and poor planning can create unnecessary pressure later. Speaking with seniors, faculty members, and specialization coordinators beforehand helps in understanding departmental expectations and hidden administrative requirements.

On host-department-specific concerns, understanding specialization-elective requirements is especially important. For students interested in Electronic Systems, the fourth-year first semester is particularly important, as that is when most specialization coursework begins. Arya strongly recommends taking the core analog design courses offered in the department and engaging with professors working in Electronic Systems as early as possible.

column3: Aditya Mehta was admitted to the Department of Physics at IIT Bombay for undergraduate studies. He pursued the traditional departmental Dual Degree program, earning a master's in theoretical condensed matter physics. Following his rigorous fourteen-month academic research project, Aditya executed a strategic career pivot. Initially preparing for international PhD programs, he eventually shifted his entire focus, successfully securing a corporate job offer in machine learning.
column4: Early departmental research sparked his interest in academia. However, by his sixth semester, Aditya recognized his CPI was below the 9.0 threshold needed for premier global PhD admissions, and his research lacked tangible outcomes. The dual degree provided a strategic, financially viable extension with minimal tuition fees, a guaranteed monthly stipend, and compulsory teaching assistant duties, allowing crucial time to deepen his research exposure.
column5: Under Professor Hridis Kumar Pal, Aditya dedicated fourteen months to a theoretical condensed matter physics project focusing on two-dimensional materials like graphene. While standard models assume non-interacting particles, his objective was to introduce strong electron-electron interactions into the theoretical framework. By mathematically modeling these dense interactions, he aimed to predict emergent magnetic phases and explain anomalous physical behaviors observed by experimentalists.
column6: Aditya developed a robust technical skillset for physics and corporate data science. He gained deep proficiency in Python for algorithmic scripting and mastered C++ for executing massive, high-speed matrix computations. He learned to deploy heavy batch codes on centralized computing clusters since laptops failed during simulations. Additionally, intense exposure to advanced linear algebra and quantum mechanics sharpened his analytical problem-solving abilities, making machine learning intuitive.
column7: The program was profoundly transformative for his career trajectory. By accelerating his mandatory coursework, Aditya enjoyed immense, unstructured freedom during his final year to focus on his master's thesis. Crucially, this surplus time allowed him to self-teach machine learning paradigms, which ultimately enabled his successful transition into the technology sector.
column8: Aditya's fourteen-month theoretical physics project yielded highly significant, publishable outcomes that validated his master's transition. His intense collaborative efforts with experimentalists successfully matched complex mathematical models to real-world observations. Consequently, this rigorous endeavor resulted in one major academic paper currently undergoing formal peer-review. Furthermore, a second distinct research paper is in active preparation, with strong expectations of international publication before his graduation.
column9: Aditya's primary challenge was psychological; despite a strong profile, he faced fifteen sequential rejections from competitive international PhD programs by December. Additionally, he navigated significant administrative friction due to a post-2021 curriculum overhaul. Since physics lacked a centralized dual-degree schedule, students independently managed project timelines and untangled widespread confusion regarding mandatory core and elective course matrices, requiring constant proactive communication with faculty advisors.
column10: Aditya advises prospective candidates to proactively identify specific research interests and secure a supportive project supervisor well before their third year concludes. Dual degree students should preemptively take extra honors courses to manage intense credit loads. Because the physics department predominantly focuses on nanoscience, strict alignment between student interests and faculty expertise is demanded. He highly recommends utilizing Institute Electives flexibly for computational courses.

column3: Taha Kachwala joined IIT Bombay as an undergraduate in Energy Science and Engineering. Leveraging the institute's structural flexibility, he opted for the Interdisciplinary Dual Degree Program, transitioning his host department to the Shailesh J. Mehta School of Management. Through this competitive pathway, Taha secured a master's specialization in operations management. Capitalizing on his newly acquired business acumen, he successfully secured a role as an Amazon operations manager.
column4: Taha's motivation was rooted in long-term strategic career planning and time optimization. Following an influential third-year operations internship, he knew he wanted corporate management roles requiring an MBA. The IDDDP presented an unparalleled opportunity to bypass the traditional, multi-year route of finishing a BTech, preparing extensively for CAT exams, and attending a separate business school, while offering elite management training and premium corporate placements.
column5: Unlike traditional engineering thesis projects, Taha's management project was a condensed, one-semester endeavor under Professor G. K. Adil. The core objective was designing a comprehensive digital simulator for complex warehouse operations utilizing Python. Crucially, Taha integrated his foundational knowledge in Energy Sciences with MBA coursework, evaluating how shifting operational management strategies directly impacted overarching sustainability metrics, tracking fluctuations in carbon emissions and localized electricity consumption.
column6: Taha's skill acquisition bridged hard technical coding and essential corporate leadership. Technically, he mastered Python to construct functional simulations of complex warehouse operations. More importantly, his immersion in SJMSOM cultivated critical soft skills absent from standard engineering curricula. He developed robust management frameworks, honed executive communication, built confidence in leading teams, and learned to rapidly adapt to shifting logistical variables and implement decisions under pressure.
column7: The IDDDP profoundly reshaped Taha's professional trajectory, solidifying his pivot from core energy engineering to corporate administration. Learning simultaneously alongside BTech peers, seasoned MBA batchmates, and elite SJMSOM professors provided unparalleled exposure to high-level managerial concepts. This unique academic synergy gave him deep, practical insights into the daily responsibilities, strategic expectations, and stress management required of an operations manager, directly facilitating his recruitment by Amazon.
column8: While Taha's master's project did not culminate in a traditional peer-reviewed academic publication, it achieved a significant level of institutional recognition and practical application. The sophisticated Python-based warehouse operational simulator he meticulously developed was deemed highly valuable. Consequently, Professor G. K. Adil is actively integrating Taha's simulator software into a brand-new elective course curriculum at SJMSOM, allowing future cohorts to actively visualize complex sustainable operations.
column9: Taha's primary challenge was surviving the intensely grueling academic workload and frantic scheduling conflicts during his fourth year. He had to simultaneously balance demanding final-year BTech courses, the rigorous foundational MBA curriculum at SJMSOM, and the notoriously aggressive B-school summer internship placement process. Additionally, the bureaucratic graduation re-tagging process proved a massive manual hassle, requiring him to physically track down signatures from multiple professors and advisors.
column10: Taha strongly advises that only students with a verified interest in management, preferably validated through prior corporate internships, should apply, as the MBA learning curve is conceptually counterintuitive for engineers. He warns that host departments enforce strict criteria; ESED restricted conversion to the top two eligible applicants. He recommends preemptively completing foundational IEOR courses and proactively contacting management professors to ensure their expectations align with curriculum.

column3: Shobhit Aggarwal commenced his undergraduate journey in the Department of Mechanical Engineering before strategically leveraging the Interdisciplinary Dual Degree Program. He transitioned into the highly innovative Koita Centre for Digital Health at IIT Bombay. Through this programmatic extension, Shobhit merged his mechanical background with advanced medical data analytics. Following his master's thesis involving clinical artificial intelligence, he successfully secured a corporate placement as a management consultant.
column4: Shobhit's primary motivation was a strong desire to radically broaden his academic horizons beyond the traditional constraints of purely mechanical engineering. Recognizing explosive global growth and immense future potential within the healthcare technology sector in India, he viewed the newly launched Koita Centre for Digital Health as an unparalleled opportunity. He strategically identified the program as a crucial gateway to acquire cross-functional expertise in artificial intelligence.
column5: Supervised by Prof. Kshitij Jadhav, Prof. Ganesh Ramakrishnan, and Dr. Hasmukh Jain, Shobhit tackled a complex oncology problem focused on advanced-stage Hodgkin Lymphoma. Doctors previously selected chemotherapy regimens based largely on subjective experience. Shobhit aimed to eliminate this guesswork by developing sophisticated AI algorithms and training Large Language Models to analyze sensitive PET-CT images, creating an accurate predictive model to determine optimal, personalized patient chemotherapy pathways.
column6: Shobhit acquired a formidable arsenal of hard technical capabilities. Entering the program with minimal software experience, he rapidly developed immense proficiency in Python programming, mastering complex data structures, algorithmic design, and data visualization tools. Because strict hospital privacy regulations prohibited commercial cloud tools, he gained the rare skill of building in-house Large Language Models. These elite quantitative skills perfectly complement the rigorous analytical demands of management consulting.
column7: The IDDDP at KCDH was incredibly transformative, providing Shobhit with the most intellectually stimulating coursework of his academic tenure. While he had always aimed for management consulting, the program fundamentally altered his trajectory by equipping him with a hardcore, advanced technical skillset. This newly acquired proficiency in AI and data science provides massive career optionality and allows him to effortlessly solve complex analytical bottlenecks in his work.
column8: Shobhit's immersion in the IDDDP unlocked unexpected avenues for elite academic recognition. He achieved a major milestone by publishing a peer-reviewed research paper detailing advanced machine learning techniques for medical data annotation and image segmentation, stemming directly from his specialized coursework. Furthermore, his primary master's thesis involving groundbreaking clinical collaboration with Tata Memorial Hospital is in its final stages and is anticipated to yield another prestigious publication.
column9: Being part of the inaugural foundational batches at the newly established KCDH presented highly specific challenges. The primary difficulty was a severe lack of programmatic precedent and a remarkably small cohort of peers conducting similar interdisciplinary medical-AI research. This isolation made technical troubleshooting exceptionally difficult without senior students to consult. Furthermore, he noted minor administrative frustrations regarding securing institutional funding to attend conferences despite having published papers.
column10: Shobhit cautions that the IDDDP is extremely rigorous; master's projects demand significantly longer hours than standard BTech projects while simultaneously juggling final-year placements. He advises applying only if fiercely passionate about the specific host department. He strongly recommends utilizing the fourth year to proactively complete foundational technical coding and statistical mathematics courses to avoid struggling, and stresses selecting faculty supervisors based entirely on problem statement alignment.

column3: Saksham comes from an Economics background and opted to pursue an Interdisciplinary Dual Degree Program (IDDDP) at the Koita Centre for Digital Health (KCDH) at IIT Bombay.
column4:
Saksham’s primary motivation was highly strategic rather than academic. He had no genuine interest in core KCDH subjects. His sole reasons for converting to the IDDDP were:

Buying Time: He needed an extra year to prepare and apply for a PhD in Economics.

Boosting CPI: Converting to a Dual Degree provides a significant CPI boost, as only Dual Degree Project (DDP) credits are counted in the 9th semester, which heavily benefits the resume submitted to the Placement Team (PT cell).
column5:
His research focused on a macroeconomic life-cycle model of the Indian economy.

Core Question: The project examined job-market entry and exit decisions, specifically analyzing how health influences those choices.

Methodology: He utilized difference equations and dynamical models, benchmarking his findings against real-world data.

Variables: The study included sensitivity analysis focusing on changes to the retirement age and other health-related parameters.
column6:
Technical Skills:

Software: Proficiency in MATLAB (used heavily for model calibration) and Stata (standard economic software).

Mathematical Modeling: Learned controlled dynamic optimization. He noted that while "real economics" relies heavily on control systems, the IITB Economics department lacks capability in this area, making this a highly valuable technical acquisition.

Machine Learning: Gained exposure to basic ML, which is a standard requirement for KCDH projects.


Soft Skills:
Cross-Institutional Collaboration: Successfully navigated working with a co-guide from ISI Delhi to get the training he needed.

Self-Direction: Managed a complex project largely independently when his primary advisor lacked domain expertise.
column7: While the IDDP program itself did not shape his academic interests (since his path was already decided), the specific project did. By designing a DDP centered on macroeconomics, he successfully aligned his KCDH workload with his future goal of pursuing a PhD in Economics. Ultimately, the program served its primary purpose: giving him the necessary time to apply for higher studies.
column8: NA: He noted that there was "not really any" formal research recognition or publications mentioned at this stage, largely because he was still waiting on final results due to the extensive time required for MATLAB calibration.
column9:
Advising Gaps: One challenge he encountered was finding domain-specific mentorship in macroeconomics. To bridge this gap, he worked closely with a co-guide from ISI Delhi, who provided specialized guidance throughout his research.

Computational Bottlenecks: Calibration in MATLAB took a significant amount of time, delaying his final results.

Poor Market Timing: He reflects that his timing was unfortunate. He noted that the PhD market is particularly brutal this year. In hindsight, he feels that pursuing a Master’s degree directly at ISI Delhi might have been a better strategic choice than the IDDDP.
column10:
When to Convert: The IDDDP is highly beneficial if you need more time to figure out your career, if you are trying to avoid a bad placement season, or if you strategically need to increase your CPI.

When Not to Convert: You must be 100% sure about wanting a Dual Degree. Do not convert just because of parental pressure; ensure the extra time is actually worth it for your specific goals, as a Master’s is not necessary for most industry domains.

On KCDH Specifically: It is a strong department with good professors spanning ML, Control Systems, Computational Biology, and Hardcore Biology. However, be prepared to use basic Machine Learning, which is generally required for the center's projects.

column3: Mohammad originally belonged to the Mechanical Engineering Dual Degree program before making the switch through the Interdisciplinary Dual Degree Program (IDDDP) to join C-MInDS (Centre for Machine Intelligence and Data Science).
column4:
Mohammad figured out fairly early that traditional mechanical engineering wasn't really his thing. What drew him in were the more technical fields like Machine Learning and AI. The IDDDP gave him a structured way to move into those areas without having to abandon the engineering foundation he'd already built.
There was also the matter of placements. Staying in the Mechanical dual-degree program would have kept his options fairly narrow, mostly general software roles, analytics, or core engineering. Switching to C-MInDS changed that considerably. It made him a genuine contender for machine learning, data science, quantitative finance, and research positions, the kind of roles that wouldn't have given a Mechanical Engineering student a second look before.
column5: His thesis, carried out under a professor affiliated with C-MInDS, centered on Market Microstructure Analysis within quantitative finance. The main goal was to understand how market microstructure shapes price formation, volatility, and trading dynamics, and then to apply those insights to trade-price prediction. What Mohammad found especially engaging was taking purely theoretical concepts and actually putting them to work in practical, data-driven settings.
column6: The project provided a rigorous technical education, equipping him with strong skills in statistics, programming, system and architecture design, quantitative finance, and formal research methodologies. The core C-MInDS coursework also significantly strengthened his mathematical and analytical foundations. On a broader professional level, he gained the invaluable soft skill of executing a complex, end-to-end project completely independently. By handling every single phase of the DDP himself-from the initial literature review and problem formulation to implementation, experimentation, and final reporting - he found that working within a team context in his professional life became significantly easier.
column7: Mohammad sees the IDDDP as one of the best academic decisions he's made, and rates the experience as extremely valuable. Before the program, he wasn't sure where his career was headed. The IDDDP helped him zero in on his interests in Quantitative Finance and Machine Learning, bridging the technical gap that a standard Mechanical Engineering degree simply couldn't. In the end, it's what made his transition into Machine Learning possible. Looking back, the one small change he'd make would be to have picked a thesis project focused purely on ML rather than quantitative finance, mostly because his current role sits closer to pure ML research. He also came away with a useful lesson about how the program actually works: he'd assumed good opportunities were gated entirely by a high CPI, but found that while CPI does matter, a lot also comes down to how actively a student networks, reaches out to professors, and goes looking for projects, specially in technical fields.
column8: Mohammad had notable research success during his time in the program. The paper tied to his thesis, written under Prof. Piyush Pandey, was accepted at the ICFMCF conference organised by the Global Association for Finance and Entrepreneurship Research (GAFER), and it's now being prepared for publication in a finance journal. On top of that, a separate project he worked on as part of a course, alongside fellow C-MInDS student Kush Patil under Prof. Arjun Bhagoji, was accepted at the AI4GoodWill workshop at ICML 2026 held in Seoul, South Korea, as well as the Trust AI European conference held in Bremen, Germany.
column9: The biggest technical challenge of his project was dealing with incomplete market data. Since exchanges like the NSE don't directly provide full order-book data, he had to reconstruct the order books manually from raw exchange data, a grueling process that took close to six months but turned out to be hugely educational. On the logistical side, time management was his toughest hurdle. He was juggling the intense pressure of placement season, sometimes sitting six or seven company tests in a single day, while at the same time handling the demanding first phase of his DDP and giving his guide regular updates. He got through it by rigorously breaking big goals down into smaller, manageable daily tasks. Administratively, the transition was smooth, and he noted that the official process for switching was fairly clear with no major issues.
column10: Mohammad highly recommends the IDDDP for students who are genuinely interested in research or higher studies, those looking to pivot into a new technical domain, or dual-degree students who feel dissatisfied with their current discipline. That said, he strongly cautions against joining without a clear/semi-clear purpose. The program demands real commitment and self-direction, and it isn't something to pursue just because the title sounds interesting. He advises juniors to explore their interests thoroughly, find professors whose research genuinely aligns with their goals, and apply only if the program serves a concrete, well-defined career or academic objective. The learning packed into this one year is second to none, but only for someone who's committed and has at least some goals in place, even if they're still a little hand-wavy.

column3: Arnav Jain is a Physics student who transitioned from a BTech to a BTech+MTech programme with a specialisation in Quantum Science and Technology. He is currently preparing to apply for a PhD in theoretical condensed matter physics.
column4: By the end of his second year, Arnav was certain he wanted to pursue research in physics but was still exploring which subfield to commit to. Two projects during his second and third years (one simulation-based and one experimental) helped him narrow his focus. Both experiences clarified what he was looking for, and by the end of his third year he had concluded that theoretical physics was his calling. The Dual Degree programme gave him the time and the right project to act on that clarity. Uncertainty around international applications also made staying on for an additional year a strategically sound choice.
column5: Arnav's master's project, supervised by Prof. Prashant Kumar, focuses on fractional quantum Hall systems - a regime where conduction occurs through exotic, fractionally charged holes that exist only within the material. He is developing analytical techniques to compute the density profiles of these holes, with findings verified by a PhD student collaborator. The work amounts to building a new framework for studying these systems, and seeing his calculations match expected results has been one of the most rewarding milestones of the project. A paper is currently in progress.
column6: Technically, Arnav has developed a strong command of analytical methods in condensed matter theory and grown considerably in his ability to work independently on open-ended problems. On the soft skills side, he has learnt to communicate complex theoretical ideas to diverse audiences, including presenting a poster at a Quantum Hall Effect conference in Mumbai. Conference participation across two to three events has also sharpened his ability to engage with the broader research community.
column7: The programme has been pivotal in helping Arnav solidify his academic identity. The additional year allowed him to pursue a project fully aligned with his interests in theoretical condensed matter, something he felt he did not have sufficient time for earlier in his degree. It also gave him the opportunity to attend conferences and build a research profile, both of which have strengthened his PhD application prospects considerably.
column8: Arnav has presented his work at a Quantum Hall Effect conference in Mumbai and attended two to three conferences in total. A research paper based on the project is currently in progress.
column9: The most significant challenge was the experience of getting stuck mid-calculation for an extended period - a common but genuinely demoralising part of theoretical research. Arnav found that persistence and patience were ultimately what carried him through, and the breakthroughs that followed made the effort worthwhile. Balancing active research with PhD application preparation has also required careful time management.
column10: Arnav encourages students to reflect carefully on whether they genuinely need the extra year before committing, and to have a clear sense of their interests and backup options by the end of third year. He recommends speaking to seniors on both sides of the decision - those who pursued the programme and those who did not, as well as consulting faculty for guidance. He also emphasises planning ahead for prerequisite courses and deadlines. Above all, he reminds students that research demands patience, and that perseverance through difficult stretches is as important as any technical skill.

column3: Jay is a Mechanical Engineering Dual Degree student placed at American Express. He decided to pursue a career in finance by the end of his third year and used the Dual Degree programme strategically to build the credentials and experience required to make that transition, switching to IDDDP in the IEOR Branch
column4: Recognising early that a standard MTech in Mechanical Engineering would not serve his goals in finance, Jay evaluated his departmental options and chose to switch to IEOR, a department whose coursework in probability and optimisation provided a strong mathematical foundation for the financial industry. Alongside his degree, he pursued CFA Level 1 and five actuarial science papers, building a set of professional credentials directly aligned with his target field.
column5: Jay's DDP project, supervised by Prof. Manjesh Hanawal, tackled the Indian Navy Ship Routing Problem, a sophisticated operational optimisation challenge. The problem involves two types of naval vessels: combat ships, which operate under fuel constraints, and shuttle ships, which serve as mobile refuelling units. The objective was to design the shuttle ship's route to minimise the overall cost of a naval mission, using mixed integer programming algorithms. The Indian Ocean context adds significant complexity compared to analogous problems solved by the US Navy, given the higher density of ships per unit area, making the project both technically demanding and practically relevant.
column6:
Throughout his project, Jay has learnt and mastered many skills: Applied knowledge in mixed integer programming and operational optimisation, alongside strong foundations in probability, quantitative finance, and actuarial science developed through coursework and professional certifications.
Three internships across the programme(EY (Actuarial Analyst), DT Portfolio Managers (Financial Analyst) and FidelFolio (Quant Investment Analyst)) developed his professional acumen and sharpened his understanding of how financial institutions operate. Managing demanding certifications alongside coursework also required considerable discipline and prioritisation.
column7: The Dual Degree programme gave Jay the time and flexibility to make a considered transition from Mechanical Engineering to finance, culminating in his placement at American Express. The combination of coursework, professional certifications, and internship experience created a well-rounded profile that stood out during the placement process.
column8: NA Jay's DDP was applied and industry-oriented in nature rather than directed toward publication or academic research output.
column9: The primary challenge was balancing mandatory coursework required to maintain his CPI with the significant demands of CFA and actuarial science preparation. Managing both simultaneously required discipline and careful prioritisation. He also navigated some administrative complexity around course retagging within IEOR, which required persistence to resolve.
column10: Maintain decent grades throughout and be deliberate about how you use the extra year. Jay recommends the programme for students who feel underprepared for placements, those who want to explore career opportunities in a different branch, and those considering a PhD or facing a challenging placement market. He also advises students to be aware of administrative processes, particularly around course retagging and faculty advisor sign-offs, and to plan for these well in advance to avoid unnecessary delays.

column3: Yash Choudhary completed his Mechanical Engineering BTech and is currently in the final year of an IDDDP in CMInDS. He is also in the early stages of building a startup in the domain of commodity finance - a reflection of the entrepreneurial direction his fifth year has helped him discover.
column4: Yash's decision to join the IDDDP came from a moment of honest self-reflection during a quantitative finance internship in his third year. The experience made him realise he was not yet ready to commit to a career path through the standard placement cycle. Rather than rushing into a decision, he chose to use the additional year at IIT Bombay to explore his interests more deliberately and build a clearer sense of where he wanted to go.
column5: Yash's research project, conducted in collaboration with SaReGaMa under a signed MOU, sits at the intersection of multimodal AI and the music industry. The work has two interlinked components: text-to-music generation, which enables users to describe emotions and ideas in natural language and receive a complete, high-fidelity musical composition in return, and music popularity prediction, which builds a system that considers factors such as composer, artist, lyricist, and social media footprint to forecast a track's commercial performance. The project was guided by Prof. Pushpak Bhattacharyya for the language and generation component and Prof. Preeti Rao for the audio and speech side. The industry collaboration and the applied, cross-disciplinary nature of the work made it a genuinely distinctive research experience.
column6: Through the project, Yash developed expertise in multimodal AI, music information retrieval, and machine learning-based prediction systems. Working across two supervisors with distinct specialisations also strengthened his ability to synthesise ideas across domains and manage a complex, multi-stakeholder research project. The experience of writing and submitting papers to conferences sharpened his academic writing and taught him the realities of the publication process.
column7: The fifth year gave Yash the space to discover his entrepreneurial inclinations and begin acting on them, something he is unlikely to have found time for in a standard placement-focused final year. The research experience and the industry collaboration with SaReGaMa have also broadened his professional network and given him a credible body of work that reflects both technical depth and real-world application.
column8: Yash authored two research papers from the project, both of which are currently available on archive. The papers have been submitted to multiple conferences in relevant domains, and the team plans to target specialised music-focused academic venues in the coming year.
column9: The academic workload of the project itself was something Yash embraced. Having completed all coursework before his fifth year began, he was able to give the research his full attention. The more personal challenge was adjusting to the experience of watching his peers graduate and move on while he continued for another year. Administratively, CMInDS (being a relatively new programme) had some structural uncertainties around course requirements and departmental procedures that required students to navigate conflicting guidance, but Yash handled this with patience and adaptability.
column10: Yash recommends the programme for students who want to build credibility for careers within India, where a CMInDS specialisation carries growing recognition among employers. For those considering a PhD, it offers strong references, a research track record, and the kind of focused output that applications require. He advises students to invest serious effort in identifying the right supervisor, speaking to lab members and understanding working styles before committing, as that relationship shapes the entire experience. He also encourages students to go in with genuine curiosity and a willingness to take the year seriously, treating it as an opportunity rather than simply an extension of their degree.

column3: Sanika Wagh transitioned from her Dual Degree in Mechanical Engineering to CMiNDS (Centre for Machine intelligence and Data Science) through the IDDDP programme. Upon graduation, she's joined Fujitsu AI, an outcome that reflects both the depth of her academic preparation and the value of the programme she chose.
column4: Sanika's engagement with AI began gradually. In her second year, she enrolled in a CS minor and later added an AI minor, drawn by the field's growing prominence and the flexibility the minor offered. Over time, the interest matured into something more purposeful, and she began orienting her coursework increasingly toward machine learning.
column5: Through the CMiNDS matching process, Sanika was assigned to Professor Parth Pandit, under whom she worked on a foundational ML problem: data subset selection. The project aimed to develop a technique for intelligently selecting subsets of data, built around kernel-based approaches in machine learning - a mathematically rigorous and experimentally demanding body of work. It involved careful problem formulation, extensive experimentation, and iterative refinement.
column6: Through her project, Sanika developed strong hands-on expertise in kernel-based ML methods, experimental design, and data subset selection techniques. The heavy mathematical component of the project pushed her to strengthen her foundations in applied mathematics as it relates to machine learning. She also gained experience in academic writing and paper submission, having submitted one paper and currently working on a second. Perhaps more importantly, Sanika credits CMiNDS with fundamentally changing her mindset. The programme instilled in her a researcher's way of thinking, how to approach problems methodically, how to ask the right questions, and how to persist through uncertainty. The culture within CMiNDS, she notes, was quite different from what she had experienced before, and it had a lasting impact on how she views and tackles challenges.
column7: Academically, the switch to CMiNDS immersed Sanika in an environment full of people working in AI and ML, giving her a level of peer exposure and academic depth that she felt would not have been possible had she simply pursued an AI project within the Mechanical department, where, she observed, AI was applied only at a surface level. Career-wise, the impact was direct and significant. The placement offer she received from Fujitsu AI was heavily influenced by both her research project and the CMiNDS programme itself. During her interview, she was able to go into considerable depth about her work - the methodology, the experiments, the challenges, the outcomes and this clearly set her apart. CMiNDS also expanded her placement opportunities: many top AI companies that recruit on campus open their roles only for CS, Electrical, and CMiNDS students, effectively placing CMiNDS students on par with CSE/Electrical in terms of placement access.
column8: She has already submitted one research paper and is currently in the process of writing and submitting a second one.
column9: There were some difficulties Sanika encountered in terms of logistics. Institute systems are largely not designed with IDDDP students in mind, often requiring additional effort for routine tasks. A key procedural point she was not fully aware of early on: IDDDP students must finalize their guide at the start of fourth year - earlier than regular DD students, and this choice is binding.
column10:
Commit fully or not at all. Switching to IDDDP means substantially stepping away from your parent department. If your interest in the new field is not deep and genuine, the transition can feel disorienting.
Invest time in choosing your guide. The guide-student relationship defines the quality of your research experience. Have substantive conversations with potential guides before deciding: understand their expectations, working style, and current projects.
Understand the process well in advance. Requirements and timelines are not always clearly communicated. Know what is expected - including prerequisites such as DS203 and DS303 - and approach the application with as much preparation as possible.
For placement aspirants, CMiNDS provides access to top AI company opportunities otherwise limited to CS and Electrical students.
For PhD aspirants, the programme offers rigorous research experience, strong faculty references, and deep integration into the AI/ML academic community, considerably more valuable, in Sanika's view, than pursuing an AI project within a non-CS home department.

column3: Vaibhav Upadhyay shifted from Aerospace Engineering to SysCon (Centre for Systems and Control) through IDDDP. He will now be pursuing a PhD at UIUC, working in mathematical control theory.
column4: He had decided early on to pursue a PhD, so his time at IITB was always research-oriented. He started a project with a SysCon professor and realized it would span nearly two years. So rather than keeping it informal, he formalized it through IDDDP. Since his guide was already from SysCon, the transition was natural. He structured his smaller projects as BTP and used the main research for DDP, making the entire journey coherent and purposeful.
column5: Under the guidance of Prof. Debasish Chatterjee at the Centre for Systems and Control, Vaibhav worked on mathematically rigorous problems in control theory. His primary research focused on reducing nonlinear dynamical systems to equivalent affine representations using tools from differential topology - a fundamental problem with broad applications across engineering systems such as aerospace, robotics, and control. Alongside his main DDP, he also worked on several collaborative side projects, including robust control of systems governed by partial differential equations and the reconstruction of degraded audio recordings, leveraging recent advances in optimization theory and classical signal processing techniques through his supervisor's international research collaborations.
column6:
Technical skills: Vaibhav developed a strong foundation in control theory, differential topology, and optimization. Through multiple research collaborations, he also gained experience in signal processing and partial differential equations.  
Soft skills: Learned to navigate the uncertainty and persistence required in long-term research. Working on a challenging two-year project taught him the importance of seeking fresh perspectives through collaborations and side projects whenever progress stalled. He also developed his scientific writing and the ability to communicate difficult mathematical ideas effectively.
column7: He has one published patent and one accepted journal paper so far, with another submitted journal paper under review, which directly strengthened his PhD applications and helped him secure a position at UIUC. SysCon's flexibility in course tagging (almost any course can be counted as a SysCon elective) gave him the freedom to pursue courses aligned with his research without rigid constraints. He also emphasized that LoRs from IDDDP guides are notably strong, especially for PhD applications, given the depth of work involved.
column8: (mentioned in impact on career itself)
column9: Vaibhav noted that SysCon's paperwork and administrative processes are relaxed, a contrast to experiences in other host departments. The main ongoing challenge was the natural difficulty of long-term research - hitting dead ends and staying motivated, which he managed through his supervisor's help by taking on side projects and international collaboration.
column10: SysCon is deeply mathematical - pursue it only if you're genuinely interested in control systems and also ready to deal with the necessary abstract mathematical framework required. If your background isn't strong and your goal is ML, consider IEOR or Cminds instead, as they offer a more accessible entry point. LoRs from IDDDP are genuinely strong for PhD applications, making it a compelling reason to consider the program if research is your goal. Structure your BTP and DDP deliberately so they build toward a coherent, strong research outcome. In SysCon, use the course flexibility to your advantage, take what genuinely supports your research.

column3: Bhavina Gajghate is currently in the fifth and final year of the Dual Degree (B.Tech + M.Tech) programme in the Department of Chemical Engineering at IIT Bombay. She is nearing graduation and is currently looking for opportunities in the chemical engineering sector.
column4: Bhavina initially planned to pursue a Joint Master's programme with Washington University in St. Louis after placements. When that opportunity did not materialize due to unforeseen circumstances, she opted for the Dual Degree at IIT Bombay to continue strengthening her research profile while earning a Master's degree and preparing for a career in chemical engineering.
column5: Under the guidance of Prof. Rochish Thaokar Bhavina worked on modelling and biomimicking the electrical behaviour of neurons. Her research focused on developing and simulating electronic circuits that replicate neuronal activity, including bursting and spiking behaviour, using mathematical neuron models and nonlinear dynamical systems. The work involved studying differential equation-based neuron models and implementing their electrical analogues to better understand neural behaviour.
column6:
Technical skills: Strengthened her knowledge of electrical engineering concepts despite coming from a chemical engineering background. She extensively used LTspice for complex circuit simulations, KiCad for PCB design, and applied programming tools such as Python and MATLAB during different stages of the project.

Soft skills: Developed independent research skills, scientific literature review, problem-solving under uncertainty, perseverance, self-learning, and the ability to proactively seek interdisciplinary collaborations and technical guidance.
column7: While Bhavina had already participated in placements during her fourth year, the Dual Degree significantly broadened her research experience and exposed her to interdisciplinary work combining chemical engineering, neuroscience, mathematics, and electrical engineering. The programme strengthened her analytical thinking, research methodology, and technical expertise, which she believes will be valuable for future industrial R&D and research-oriented roles.
column8: The project has produced novel research results. However, due to limited time before graduation, the work has not yet been converted into a publication. Bhavina hopes to continue collaborating with her guide after graduation and eventually publish the findings.
column9: One of the biggest challenges was working on an interdisciplinary research problem with little prior work to reference, making it difficult to judge whether the chosen approach was correct. Since the project involved significant electrical engineering concepts, finding departmental expertise was sometimes challenging, requiring her to independently seek help from faculty and students outside her department. She also noted that the Dual Degree journey can feel isolating because students often lack the peer support systems available during B.Tech tenure. From an administrative perspective, she felt there should be a centralized resource addressing common queries for Dual Degree students, although her department has recently started creating a handbook for future batches.
column10: Bhavina recommends the Dual Degree programme to students interested in pursuing a PhD, especially those aiming to build a strong research profile through publications. She advises students to carefully choose both their research topic and supervisor, ensuring they align with long-term academic or career goals. Students interested in industry should look for projects with industrial collaborations, while those considering Europe for higher studies may benefit from the Master's qualification offered by the Dual Degree programme. Before selecting a guide, she strongly recommends speaking with seniors who have worked under the professor to understand their mentoring style and research environment. She also highlights Prof. Abhijit Majumder, Prof. Guruswamy (polymers and microplastics), and faculty working in bioengineering as researchers worth considering based on individual interests.

column3: Nilabha Saha is pursuing the Interdisciplinary Dual Degree Programme (IDDDP) with a B.Tech in Computer Science and Engineering and an M.Sc. in Mathematics. He originally joined IIT Bombay in Engineering Physics, later branch changed to CSE, and subsequently converted to the IDDDP to pursue advanced mathematics. After graduation, he will be joining a quantitative finance firm in a software-oriented role.
column4: Nilabha's primary motivation for joining the IDDDP stemmed from his strong interest in mathematics. During a research internship, he was advised that formal mathematics coursework on his transcript would strengthen his academic profile, which prompted him to pursue a Mathematics minor. As he progressed through the minor alongside courses in Statistics and Informatics, he realized that it did not provide the depth and breadth of mathematical training he was seeking. This led him to join the Mathematics IDDDP, allowing him to build a more rigorous foundation and gain a deeper understanding of advanced mathematical concepts.
column5: Unlike most departments, the Mathematics department does not have a traditional Master's thesis for IDDDP students. Instead, Nilabha completed four independent project courses under different faculty members rather than working on a single unified research problem. His interests lie in algebra, number theory, and algebraic geometry, but due to the advanced nature of these fields, the projects focused primarily on advanced mathematical learning rather than original research. He also highlighted the need for dedicated IDDDP project courses within the department to streamline the process for future students.
column6:
Technical skills: Developed strong foundations in higher mathematics, particularly algebra and related areas, which have strengthened his understanding of fields such as mathematical cryptography and theoretical computer science.

Soft skills: Improved logical reasoning, structured thinking, problem-solving, persistence, and administrative navigation. Managing repeated registration and academic administration challenges also taught him patience, resilience, and how to effectively communicate with different administrative offices to resolve complex issues.
column7: Nilabha emphasized that he chose the IDDDP purely out of academic interest rather than for career advantages. While the programme itself did not directly contribute to securing his current job in quantitative finance, it provided the mathematical training he was seeking. The decision came at the cost of losing a potential pre-placement offer due to extending his degree by one year, although he ultimately secured a better opportunity.
column8: Since the Mathematics department does not require a traditional Master's thesis, Nilabha did not pursue a research publication or thesis defence as part of the programme. His work primarily consisted of advanced coursework and independent mathematical projects rather than publishable research.
column9: Nilabha identified administrative issues as the biggest challenge throughout the IDDDP. Frequent manual registrations, manual course retagging, unclear graduation requirements, inconsistent information across departments, and limited awareness of the IDDDP among faculty and administrative staff created significant hurdles each semester. He also noted that many academic systems are not designed to support IDDDP students, requiring constant follow-ups with multiple offices. Academically, he found it difficult to plan an ideal course sequence without structured guidance, particularly in balancing breadth and depth within mathematics.
column10: Nilabha recommends the IDDDP primarily to students who are genuinely passionate about a subject or intend to pursue higher studies. For students targeting research or a PhD, the programme offers substantial academic value at a relatively low cost. However, students aiming directly for industry should carefully consider the trade-offs, including administrative complexity, delayed graduation, possible impact on internships and PPOs, and reduced awareness of the programme among recruiters. For Mathematics specifically, he strongly advises students to review the department's eligibility criteria early and ensure they complete the required prerequisite courses before the end of their third year.

column3: Yashas Saliyan is a fifth-year Dual Degree student in the Department of Electrical Engineering, specializing in Electronic Systems. After graduation, he will be joining the industry in an FPGA-related role.
column4: Yashas opted for the Dual Degree programme for two reasons. Firstly, he wanted to explore research after finding industrial VLSI work during his internship less engaging than the research-oriented projects he had undertaken at IIT Bombay. He was particularly interested in working under a leading researcher in computer architecture from the CSE department to gain exposure to world-class research before deciding whether to pursue higher studies. Secondly, he felt that an additional Master's degree would strengthen his placement prospects in core electrical engineering, especially since he was uncertain about securing a PPO and believed the extra year would improve both his technical expertise and academic performance.
column5: Yashas is jointly supervised by Prof. Biswabandan Panda (Computer Science & Engineering) and Prof. Sachin Patkar (Electrical Engineering). His research lies in the domain of computer architecture. The project focuses on optimizing address translation hardware to minimize the performance impact of the overheads introduced by virtual memory in some recent data-intensive workloads. Prior works propose to repurpose underutilized levels of the cache hierarchy to speed up address translation; the project attempts to refine these proposals to maximize the utility derived from the repurposed cache.
column6:
Technical skills: Developed strong proficiency in C++ and Python, gained experience working with large open-source codebases, architecture simulators, and data analysis tools, and strengthened his understanding of computer architecture and performance evaluation methodologies.

Soft skills: Learned systematic problem-solving, scientific thinking, technical writing, presentation skills, and the importance of thorough experimentation and validation in research. Working closely with experienced researchers also gave him valuable insight into conducting high-quality research.
column7: The Dual Degree programme helped Yashas make a well-informed career decision by giving him first-hand exposure to academic research. While he developed immense respect for research and the people working in the field, he realized that pursuing research full-time may not currently align with his career aspirations. Instead, the programme allowed him to objectively compare industry and research before committing to either path. He believes this realistic understanding of research is one of the most valuable outcomes of his Dual Degree experience.
column9: One major administrative challenge arose from having supervisors across two departments, requiring Yashas to independently coordinate examiners, room bookings, and other logistics. As someone who considers himself less outgoing, coordinating multiple faculty members proved demanding but also helped him improve his communication and organizational skills. Academically, balancing an intensive research project with placement preparation was particularly challenging. Adapting to the expectations and pace of a serious research environment, while simultaneously preparing for placements, required significant effort and perseverance.
column10:
Yashas recommends the Dual Degree programme to students who are genuinely undecided between research and industry, as it provides valuable research exposure before making long-term career decisions. However, students who already possess extensive research experience and are certain about pursuing a PhD may not require the additional year. His strongest advice is to carefully choose a highly active research guide working in one's area of interest and to evaluate both the quality of research and the lab environment before joining, as these factors largely determine the value of the Dual Degree experience. He also recommends referring to the blog written by alumnus Anubhav Bhatla, who documented the Dual Degree conversion process and successfully pursued high-impact research before joining MIT for his PhD.

The link for the blog: https://anubhavbhatla.github.io/blog/2024/dual-degree/

column3: Sanuraag Mishra is an IDDDP student in the Centre for Machine Intelligence and Data Science (C-MInDS). He will be graduating this year and has secured a Pre-Placement Offer (PPO), after which he plans to join the corporate sector. He also notes that C-MInDS offers excellent opportunities for students interested in Machine Learning and Data Science, both in research and placements.
column4: Sanuraag believes the primary motivation for joining the IDDDP should be the opportunity to work with highly accomplished faculty members and pursue meaningful research. He chose the programme to learn under leading researchers at C-MInDS and gain exposure to cutting-edge machine learning research. While he acknowledges that the programme also improves placement prospects due to the growing demand for AI/ML roles, he considers research opportunities and faculty mentorship to be the main reasons for opting for the programme.
column5: Under the guidance of Prof. Usha Ananthakumar (School of Management), Sanuraag worked on applying Machine Learning to real-world business problems. His research focuses on addressing covariate shift during model deployment, where data distributions differ between training and testing environments. His work investigates the role of decision boundaries in improving model performance under distribution shifts through extensive experimentation across multiple machine learning models. The project bridges theoretical ML research with practical deployment challenges, making it highly applicable to real-world business and industrial settings.
column6:
