Technical skills: Developed expertise in machine learning research methodology, large-scale experimentation, identifying research gaps, scientific writing, and publishing research papers. He also gained practical experience in designing deployable AI solutions with real-world applications.

Soft skills: Improved his ability to communicate complex technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders, articulate research ideas clearly, collaborate with researchers, and frame research in terms of practical business impact rather than purely mathematical novelty.
column7: Sanuraag considers the IDDDP one of the best academic decisions he has made. The programme provided direct exposure to advanced AI and Machine Learning research while significantly strengthening his industry profile. He believes the research experience, faculty mentorship, and specialized coursework collectively enhanced both his technical depth and placement opportunities in the rapidly growing AI/ML industry.
column8: His research has already resulted in significant academic recognition. One paper was presented at the 5th NCMLI Conference, where it was ranked among the Top 3 papers out of more than 100 submissions. The team has subsequently submitted another paper to the DSA Conference and is also preparing a journal publication, making the research output highly active and publication-oriented.
column9: Sanuraag describes identifying meaningful research problems as the biggest challenge. Research often produces unexpected or unsuccessful results, requiring repeated experimentation before arriving at useful conclusions. When results did not match expectations, he relied heavily on discussions with his guide, who connected him with other researchers to refine ideas and identify new directions. From a programme perspective, he also highlighted administrative challenges such as unclear course tagging, evolving curriculum requirements, and ambiguity regarding elective baskets, which required frequent coordination with programme administrators.
column10: Sanuraag strongly recommends the programme to students genuinely interested in Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence, and research. He advises students to begin interacting with prospective faculty members early—preferably through summer internships, semester projects, or course projects—since prior interaction significantly improves the chances of securing a thesis under a professor. He also recommends choosing a research topic aligned with long-term interests rather than selecting the programme solely for placements. While the coursework is demanding, particularly for students transitioning from other departments, he believes the exposure to outstanding faculty, research opportunities, and AI/ML applications makes the effort worthwhile. For students from non-C-MInDS departments, he also suggests proactively discussing curriculum mapping with their home department to reduce unnecessary coursework wherever possible.

column3: Abeer Mishra initially pursued a B.Tech in Engineering Physics before converting to the Interdisciplinary Dual Degree Programme (IDDDP) in Electrical Engineering with a specialization in Electronic Systems. He graduated last year and is currently working in the AI Software Team at Qualcomm
column4: Abeer decided to pursue the IDDDP while interning at McMaster, where he worked on biomedical imaging research. The internship strengthened his interest in machine learning and signal processing, and he wanted to continue working in this area through his Dual Degree project. He also hoped to gain exposure to electrical hardware, although his eventual project focused primarily on algorithms, machine learning, and signal processing.
column5: Under the guidance of Prof. Vikram Gadre, Abeer worked on developing contactless biometric recognition using mobile cameras in collaboration with UIDAI. The project aimed to enable fingerprint authentication using photographs captured through a smartphone camera instead of conventional contact-based fingerprint scanners. The primary research challenge involved matching contactless fingerprint images with existing contact-based Aadhaar fingerprint databases by developing robust image processing, signal processing, and machine learning algorithms capable of bridging the domain gap between the two image types.
column6:
Technical skills: Developed expertise in machine learning, signal processing, image processing, Android app development, Python, Java, Kotlin, and MATLAB while building both the recognition algorithms and the mobile application prototype.

Soft skills: Learned project management, cross-team collaboration, stakeholder communication, mentoring junior interns, handling technical feedback from senior researchers, and coordinating with multiple organizations including UIDAI and academic collaborators.
column7: The IDDDP gave Abeer hands-on exposure to modern machine learning research and significantly changed his understanding of how research is conducted beyond classroom coursework. Working on a real-world deployment problem allowed him to bridge theoretical concepts with practical applications while also making him eligible for Electrical Engineering placement opportunities, which ultimately helped him secure his position at Qualcomm.
column8: The project was demonstrated at the Global Fintech Fest as part of UIDAI's technology showcase after the team successfully developed the first working prototype of the application. Multiple papers emerged from the broader project team, with one paper under review during the interview. Abeer also led the application development component and presented the work during technical demonstrations.
column9: The central research challenge involved matching contactless fingerprint images with conventional fingerprint databases despite significant domain differences between the two image types. From a programme perspective, Abeer felt that the IDDDP rules and course requirements were not clearly documented when he joined, creating uncertainty regarding programme regulations. He also noted that interdisciplinary projects require extensive coordination between different stakeholders, making communication and people management just as important as technical ability.
column10: Abeer advises students to choose an IDDDP solely because they are genuinely interested in the department and research area, rather than for the department tag or placement advantages. Since the coursework and research are demanding, students who are not intrinsically interested may find the programme mentally exhausting. He recommends selecting projects that align with one's interests, exploring research before committing, and approaching faculty members early. For students interested in Electrical Engineering, he particularly recommends exploring research opportunities under Prof. Udayan Ganguly, whose work in neuromorphic engineering provides excellent opportunities for undergraduate researchers.

column3: Aditi Chintey pursued the Dual Degree (DD) programme in Visual Communication at IDC, IIT Bombay. Having completed the programme, she secured a placement as a Design Analyst at JPMorgan. Reflecting on her experience, she believes the DD programme provided valuable opportunities to specialise in her chosen design discipline while allowing her to build a stronger portfolio and engage in in-depth project work.
column4: Aditi chose the DD programme because she had always envisioned pursuing a Master's degree and wanted to specialise further within Visual Communication. The programme offered an efficient pathway to complete both degrees while gaining advanced design exposure. She also felt that a Master's qualification would strengthen her placement prospects, making the DD route a natural choice.
column5: During the programme, Aditi worked on a children's book inspired by the folk art and folktales of Majuli Island in Assam. The project explored how the culture and folk traditions of Assam could be made more accessible to children through design. Beyond developing the visual language of the book, she focused on aspects such as storytelling, typography, print production, material selection, sustainability, and the broader social purpose of design. The project also gave her the opportunity to work closely with multiple faculty mentors and explore communication design from a new perspective.
column6:
Technical skills: Aditi deepened her understanding of communication design, print production, typography, illustration, publication design, and user-centred design considerations. She also gained exposure to sustainable design practices and learned to evaluate design decisions from both creative and practical perspectives. 

Soft skills: She developed stronger critical thinking, reflection, time management, and communication skills. The DD environment also encouraged her to interact with people from diverse backgrounds, improving her networking abilities, adaptability, and interpersonal skills.
column7: The DD programme gave Aditi the time and flexibility to focus deeply on a single design project without the burden of additional coursework. This allowed her to build a stronger portfolio, work more closely with faculty members, and refine her design thinking. She believes the experience prepared her well for placements and broadened her perspective on design as a socially responsible discipline, ultimately helping her secure a role at JPMorgan.
column8: The children's book has not yet been formally published. However, one of Aditi's professors encouraged her to refine the work and submit it to publishing houses. Although exchange commitments and placements delayed the process, she intends to revisit the project in the future and explore publication opportunities.
column9: The biggest challenge was balancing the demanding project timeline with placement preparation, portfolio development, exchange-related applications, and visa formalities. Although the DD programme reduced coursework compared to the Bachelor's programme, managing multiple major commitments simultaneously required careful planning and effective time management. Looking back, she recommends starting preparations early and maintaining a structured schedule throughout the semester.
column10: Aditi believes the DD programme is best suited for students who genuinely enjoy learning, wish to specialise further, and value the opportunity to work closely with professors on an in-depth project. Students looking to strengthen their portfolio, pursue research or exchange opportunities, or spend more time refining their design practice will benefit greatly from the programme. She also encourages students to make full use of IDC's diverse elective courses, workshops, and faculty expertise by exploring subjects beyond their comfort zone and choosing courses that make design enjoyable, not just career-oriented.
